title: 全球变暖与干旱性质改变文献验证
objective: 验证 ZhiZhi 对全球变暖导致干旱性质、频率、强度、持续时间和生态影响改变的文献检索、结构化提取和知识缺口探测能力。

papergraph.title: A Multidecadal Assessment of Drought Intensification in the Middle East and North Africa: The Role of Global Warming and Rainfall Deficit
papergraph.citation: A. Kenawy et al. (2025) A Multidecadal Assessment of Drought Intensification in the Middle East and North Africa: The Role of Global Warming and Rainfall Deficit doi:10.1007/s41748-025-00576-4
papergraph.abstract: This study provides a comprehensive assessment of drought distribution and severity across the Middle East and North Africa (MENA) region from 1981 to 2021, utilizing two widely recognized drought indices: the Standardized Precipitation Index (SPI) and the Standardized Precipitation Evapotranspiration Index (SPEI). We employed the CRU TS v. 4.07 datasets to assess changes in drought characteristics (e.g., frequency, intensity, duration, and severity) at the 12-months timescale for the main Koppen-Geiger climate zones in the region. Results indicate a significant shift from wetter conditions before 1998 to more frequent drought occurrences post-1998, particularly in northern MENA, including North Africa, the Levant, and western parts of the Arabian Peninsula, northern Iraq, and western Iran. Both the SPI and SPEI indicate significant positive changes in drought frequency across 19.4% of the MENA region during the period 1998–2021, compared to 12.9% in the earlier period of 1981–1997. The SPEI, which incorporates the effects of evapotranspiration, demonstrated a more pronounced increase in drought frequency and severity than the SPI, reflecting the growing impact of rising temperatures in the most recent decades. The region experienced more frequent and prolonged, but less intense, droughts in the past two decades. Spatially, the SPEI highlighted significant drought intensification in temperate and cold desert (CWb, BWk) zones across the region, primarily driven by increasing air temperatures. On the other hand, SPI tended to capture well the mild and moderate drought events, while it underestimated the severity of the most anomalous dry events (i.e., severe, extreme, and very extreme). For example, in comparison to the SPI, the SPEI indicated an increase of 15% in the areas affected by severe to very extreme droughts in arid regions (Aw). The study also noted discrepancies between the SPI and SPEI in hyper-arid regions like the Sahara and Arabian Peninsula (BWh), emphasizing the importance of using temperature-inclusive indices like SPEI for more accurate drought assessments in warmer and water-limited climates in the region. Our findings indicate that the SPEI can be seen as a more reliable indicator for understanding drought severity in the region in the context of global warming. Overall, our findings emphasize the urgency of adopting adaptation and mitigation strategies to address the increasing frequency and severity of droughts due to global warming.
strengths: Use of two complementary drought indices (SPI and SPEI)
Application of CRU TS v. 4.07 datasets over a multidecadal period (1981–2021)
Spatial analysis across Koppen-Geiger climate zones
Explicit attribution of drought intensification to rising temperatures
improvements: Incorporation of higher-resolution climate data for localized drought assessment
Integration of socioeconomic vulnerability metrics to assess drought impacts
Extension of analysis beyond 2021 to capture recent extreme events
papergraph.scenario: Drought intensification in the Middle East and North Africa (MENA) region under global warming, focusing on shifts in drought characteristics pre- and post-1998.
papergraph.benchmark: 4.07 datasets to assess changes in drought characteristics (e.g., frequency, intensity, duration, and severity) at the 12-months timescale for the main Koppen-Geiger climate zones in the region.
papergraph.contribution: Demonstrates that SPEI is a more reliable indicator than SPI for assessing drought severity in the MENA region under global warming, especially in warm, water-limited climates; reveals spatial patterns of drought intensification linked to rising temperatures in temperate and cold desert zones; quantifies increased areal extent of severe-to-very-extreme droughts in arid regions using SPEI.

papergraph.title: Potential impacts of 1.5 °C and 2 °C global warming levels on drought modes over Eastern Africa
papergraph.citation: M. Nguvava et al. (2023) Potential impacts of 1.5 °C and 2 °C global warming levels on drought modes over Eastern Africa doi:10.1007/s10584-023-03631-z
papergraph.abstract: This study examines the impacts of 1.5 °C and 2.0 °C global warming levels (GWLs) on the characteristics of four major drought modes over Eastern Africa in the future under two climate forcing scenarios (RCP4.5 and RCP8.5). The droughts were quantified using two drought indices: the standardized precipitation evapotranspiration index (SPEI) and the standardized precipitation index (SPI) at 12-month scale. Four major drought modes were identified with the principal component analysis (PCA). Multi-model simulation datasets from the Coordinated Regional Climate Downscaling Experiment (CORDEX) were analysed for the study. The skill of the models to reproduce the spatial distribution and frequency of past drought modes over Eastern Africa was examined by comparing the simulated results with the Climate Research Unit (CRU) observation. The models give realistic simulations of the historical drought modes over the region. The correlation between the simulated and observed spatial pattern of the drought modes is high ( r ≥ 0.7). Over the hotspot of the drought modes, the observed drought frequency is within the simulated values, and the simulations agree with the observation that the frequency of SPI-12 droughts is less than that of SPEI-12 droughts. For both RCP4.5 and RCP8.5 scenarios, the simulation ensemble projects no changes in the spatial structure of the drought modes but suggests an increase in SPEI-12 drought intensity and frequency over the hotspots of the drought modes. The magnitude of the increase, which varies over the drought mode hotspots, is generally higher at 2 °C than at 1.5 °C global warming levels. More than 75% of the simulations agree on these projections. The projections also show that the increase in drought intensity and frequency is more from increased potential evapotranspiration than from reduced precipitation. Hence, the study suggests that to reduce impacts of global warming on future drought, the adaptation activities should focus on reducing evaporative loss surface water.
papergraph.method: Principal component analysis (PCA) applied to multi-model simulation datasets from the Coordinated Regional Climate Downscaling Experiment (CORDEX); droughts quantified using standardized precipitation evapotranspiration index (SPEI) and standardized precipitation index (SPI) at 12-month scale; model skill assessed by comparing simulated results with Climate Research Unit (CRU) observations.
papergraph.scenario: RCP4.5 and RCP8.5 climate forcing scenarios
papergraph.contribution: Projects no change in spatial structure of drought modes but increased SPEI-12 drought intensity and frequency over hotspots under 1.5 °C and 2 °C global warming levels; identifies increased potential evapotranspiration—not reduced precipitation—as primary driver; recommends adaptation focused on reducing evaporative loss of surface water.
credibility_reasons: credibility multiplied down by low publication quality

papergraph.title: Drought type determines the pattern of ecological response to drought in trees with different sensitivities
papergraph.citation: Xuge Wang et al. (2025) Drought type determines the pattern of ecological response to drought in trees with different sensitivities doi:10.1007/s11104-025-07365-y
papergraph.contribution: Title: Drought type determines the pattern of ecological response to drought in trees with different sensitivities Venue: Plant and Soil Year: 2025 Citation: Xuge Wang et al.

papergraph.title: Analysis of the Impact of Drought Characteristics Change on Natural Ecological Environment in the West of Hexi Corridor
papergraph.citation: Chong Zhang et al. (2024) Analysis of the Impact of Drought Characteristics Change on Natural Ecological Environment in the West of Hexi Corridor doi:10.1051/e3sconf/202452001014
papergraph.abstract: The plant communities and wild animals in the Gobi desert outside the oases in the west of Hexi Corridor are an indispensable and important part of the human living environment. Affected by global warming, the spatial and temporal distribution patterns of precipitation have changed significantly. This article uses daily precipitation data from 6 hydro-logical stations in the middle and lower reaches of the Shule River Basin from 1957 to 2018, and daily precipitation data from 3 meteorological stations from 1980 to 2018. Through research on annual scale precipitation anomaly percentage, effective precipitation days and annual distribution, and drought station frequency ratio and other parameters, it is shown that there is a significant change in precipitation distribution within the year. The distribution of effective precipitation is not conducive to the survival of ecosystems, and seasonal drought is on the rise. The trend of continuous drought in two seasons and three seasons is on the rise, and the evolution of drought has a significant impact on the natural ecological environment. Effective rainfall is a key element in the rise and fall, distribution, and change of natural ecosystems. Studying the impact of changes in drought characteristics on natural ecology is of great significance for actively responding to drought disasters and maintaining basic ecology.
papergraph.scenario: Gobi desert outside oases in the west of Hexi Corridor, affected by global warming and changing precipitation patterns.
papergraph.contribution: Shows significant intra-annual change in precipitation distribution; identifies that ineffective distribution of effective precipitation harms ecosystem survival; documents rising seasonal, bi-seasonal, and tri-seasonal drought trends; highlights effective rainfall as a key driver of natural ecosystem dynamics.

keynotes.title: A Multidecadal Assessment of Drought Intensification in the Middle East and North Africa: The Role of Global Warming and Rainfall Deficit

keynote.title: A Multidecadal Assessment of Drought Intensification in the Middle East and North Africa: The Role of Global Warming and Rainfall Deficit
keynote.core_problem: Assessing how drought characteristics (frequency, intensity, duration, severity) have changed across the MENA region from 1981–2021, and evaluating the relative utility of temperature-inclusive vs. precipitation-only drought indices under global warming.
contributions: Quantified multidecadal shifts in drought metrics across MENA climate zones using SPI and SPEI.
Identified a post-1998 intensification trend—especially in northern MENA—with spatially heterogeneous patterns.
Demonstrated SPEI’s superior sensitivity to warming-driven drought intensification compared to SPI, particularly in temperate/cold desert (CWb, BWk) and arid (Aw) zones.
Documented systematic underestimation of severe-to-extreme droughts by SPI in arid regions, with SPEI showing +15% areal increase for such events in Aw zones.
Highlighted index divergence in hyper-arid (BWh) regions, underscoring limitations of precipitation-only metrics in warming, water-limited climates.
methods: Trend analysis of drought frequency, intensity, duration, and severity at 12-month timescale.
Use of Standardized Precipitation Index (SPI) and Standardized Precipitation Evapotranspiration Index (SPEI).
Spatial analysis stratified by Koppen-Geiger climate zones (e.g., CWb, BWk, Aw, BWh).
Temporal segmentation into pre-1998 (1981–1997) and post-1998 (1998–2021) periods.
experiments_or_evidence: SPI and SPEI computed from CRU TS v. 4.07 gridded meteorological data (1981–2021).
Reported significant positive change in drought frequency across 19.4% of MENA (1998–2021) vs. 12.9% (1981–1997).
SPEI showed more pronounced increases in drought frequency and severity than SPI, especially in CWb/BWk zones.
SPEI indicated 15% greater area affected by severe-to-very-extreme droughts in Aw zones compared to SPI.
Qualitative and quantitative comparison of SPI/SPEI behavior across climate zones, including divergence in BWh zones.
assumptions: CRU TS v. 4.07 adequately represents regional climate variability for drought index computation.
12-month timescale is appropriate for capturing climatologically relevant drought dynamics in MENA.
Koppen-Geiger classification provides a meaningful framework for spatial stratification of drought responses.
SPEI’s incorporation of PET (via Thornthwaite method) sufficiently captures warming-driven evaporative demand changes.
limitations: Reliance on CRU TS v. 4.07 gridded data may obscure local-scale variability.
Limited representation of hyper-arid regions (BWh) where SPI and SPEI diverge significantly.
Absence of soil moisture or groundwater data integration.
datasets_or_materials: CRU TS v. 4.07 gridded meteorological dataset (precipitation, temperature).

important_claims.claim: SPEI is a more reliable indicator than SPI for assessing drought severity in MENA under global warming.
important_claims.evidence: SPEI demonstrated more pronounced increases in drought frequency and severity than SPI, especially in CWb/BWk zones driven by rising air temperatures; it captured +15% more area under severe-to-very-extreme drought in Aw zones.

important_claims.claim: Drought frequency increased significantly post-1998 across 19.4% of MENA, versus 12.9% pre-1998.
important_claims.evidence: Trend analysis of SPI and SPEI at 12-month timescale using CRU TS v. 4.07 data showed statistically significant positive changes in drought frequency in the later period.

important_claims.claim: SPI underestimates severity of severe-to-extreme droughts in arid (Aw) regions.
important_claims.evidence: Compared to SPI, SPEI indicated a 15% increase in area affected by severe, extreme, and very extreme droughts in Aw zones.

important_claims.claim: SPI and SPEI diverge notably in hyper-arid (BWh) regions like the Sahara and Arabian Peninsula.
important_claims.evidence: The study explicitly noted discrepancies between the two indices in BWh zones, emphasizing the need for temperature-inclusive metrics in such climates.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Provides empirical basis for selecting drought indices in warming-prone, water-limited regions; highlights climate-zone-specific index performance; identifies CRU-based benchmark trends for model evaluation and adaptation planning in MENA.

keynotes.title: Potential impacts of 1.5 °C and 2 °C global warming levels on drought modes over Eastern Africa

keynote.title: Potential impacts of 1.5 °C and 2 °C global warming levels on drought modes over Eastern Africa
keynote.core_problem: How 1.5 °C and 2.0 °C global warming levels affect the spatial structure, intensity, and frequency of four major drought modes over Eastern Africa under different climate scenarios.
contributions: Quantifies differential impacts of 1.5 °C vs. 2.0 °C global warming on drought modes in Eastern Africa
Identifies four major drought modes using PCA applied to SPEI-12 and SPI-12 indices
Evaluates multi-model CORDEX ensemble skill in reproducing historical drought modes against CRU observations
Attributes projected increases in drought intensity/frequency primarily to increased potential evapotranspiration rather than reduced precipitation
methods: Principal component analysis (PCA) to identify four major drought modes
Use of standardized precipitation evapotranspiration index (SPEI-12) and standardized precipitation index (SPI-12)
Multi-model ensemble analysis from Coordinated Regional Climate Downscaling Experiment (CORDEX)
Model evaluation via spatial pattern correlation (r ≥ 0.7) and drought frequency comparison with CRU observational data
experiments_or_evidence: High correlation (r ≥ 0.7) between simulated and observed spatial patterns of drought modes
Observed drought frequency falls within simulated range at hotspots
Consistent finding across >75% of simulations: increased SPEI-12 drought intensity and frequency at hotspots under both RCP4.5 and RCP8.5
Greater increase in drought intensity/frequency at 2 °C vs. 1.5 °C GWL
Attribution evidence: increase driven more by increased potential evapotranspiration than reduced precipitation
assumptions: CORDEX multi-model ensemble adequately represents regional climate dynamics relevant to drought modes
Historical model skill (validated against CRU) implies robustness for future projections
SPEI-12 and SPI-12 are appropriate metrics for characterizing drought modes in Eastern Africa
datasets_or_materials: Coordinated Regional Climate Downscaling Experiment (CORDEX) multi-model simulation datasets
Climate Research Unit (CRU) observational dataset (for historical validation)

important_claims.claim: No changes in the spatial structure of the four major drought modes are projected under 1.5 °C or 2.0 °C global warming.
important_claims.evidence: Simulation ensemble projects no changes in the spatial structure of the drought modes.

important_claims.claim: SPEI-12 drought intensity and frequency increase over drought mode hotspots at both 1.5 °C and 2.0 °C global warming levels, with larger increases at 2.0 °C.
important_claims.evidence: The magnitude of the increase is generally higher at 2 °C than at 1.5 °C; more than 75% of simulations agree on these projections.

important_claims.claim: Projected increases in drought intensity and frequency are driven more by increased potential evapotranspiration than by reduced precipitation.
important_claims.evidence: The projections also show that the increase in drought intensity and frequency is more from increased potential evapotranspiration than from reduced precipitation.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Provides a methodological template for linking global warming levels to regional drought mode changes using PCA + SPEI/SPI, validated multi-model ensembles (CORDEX), and attribution analysis—enabling transfer to other regions or hazards.

keynotes.title: Analysis of the Impact of Drought Characteristics Change on Natural Ecological Environment in the West of Hexi Corridor

keynote.title: Analysis of the Impact of Drought Characteristics Change on Natural Ecological Environment in the West of Hexi Corridor
keynote.core_problem: How changing drought characteristics—driven by altered precipitation patterns under global warming—affect the natural ecological environment (plant communities and wild animals) in the Gobi desert region west of the Hexi Corridor.
contributions: Quantified temporal shifts in precipitation distribution using long-term station data
Identified rising trends in multi-seasonal droughts (two- and three-season continuous drought)
Linked changes in effective precipitation timing/distribution to ecosystem vulnerability
methods: Analysis of annual-scale precipitation anomaly percentage
Calculation and trend analysis of effective precipitation days and their annual distribution
Computation of drought station frequency ratio
Use of daily precipitation data from hydrological and meteorological stations over multi-decade periods
experiments_or_evidence: Trend analysis showing significant change in intra-annual precipitation distribution
Evidence that effective precipitation distribution is increasingly misaligned with ecosystem needs
Observed rise in frequency of two-season and three-season continuous drought events
datasets_or_materials: Daily precipitation data from 6 hydrological stations in the middle and lower reaches of the Shule River Basin (1957–2018)
Daily precipitation data from 3 meteorological stations (1980–2018)

important_claims.claim: The distribution of effective precipitation is not conducive to the survival of ecosystems.
important_claims.evidence: Analysis of effective precipitation days and annual distribution shows misalignment with ecosystem requirements.

important_claims.claim: Seasonal drought is on the rise, with increasing trends in two-season and three-season continuous drought.
important_claims.evidence: Drought station frequency ratio and temporal trend analysis reveal rising multi-seasonal drought persistence.

important_claims.claim: Changes in drought characteristics significantly impact the natural ecological environment.
important_claims.evidence: Observed correlation between evolving drought patterns (e.g., timing, duration, frequency) and ecosystem stress in Gobi desert plant communities and wildlife.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Provides a regional empirical framework for linking long-term precipitation metrics (anomaly %, effective days, station frequency ratio) to ecological vulnerability—applicable to arid-zone drought-ecology studies where station-based climate data exists but remote sensing or in-situ ecological monitoring is sparse.

main_methods: Principal component analysis (PCA) applied to multi-model simulation datasets from the Coordinated Regional Climate Downscaling Experiment (CORDEX); droughts quantified using standardized precipitation evapotranspiration index (SPEI) and standardized precipitation index (SPI) at 12-month scale; model skill assessed by comparing simulated results with Climate Research Unit (CRU) observations.
main_scenarios: Drought intensification in the Middle East and North Africa (MENA) region under global warming, focusing on shifts in drought characteristics pre- and post-1998.
Gobi desert outside oases in the west of Hexi Corridor, affected by global warming and changing precipitation patterns.
RCP4.5 and RCP8.5 climate forcing scenarios
main_benchmarks: 4.07 datasets to assess changes in drought characteristics (e.g., frequency, intensity, duration, and severity) at the 12-months timescale for the main Koppen-Geiger climate zones in the region.

The skill of the models to reproduce the spatial distribution and frequency of past drought modes over Eastern Africa was examined by comparing the simulated results with the Climate Research Unit (CRU) observation.
The correlation between the simulated and observed spatial pattern of the drought modes is high ( r ≥ 0.7).

Over the hotspot of the drought modes, the observed drought frequency is within the simulated values, and the simulations agree with the observation that the frequency of SPI-12 droughts is less than that of SPEI-12 droughts.

For both RCP4.5 and RCP8.5 scenarios, the simulation ensemble projects no changes in the spatial structure of the drought modes but suggests an increase in SPEI-12 drought intensity and frequency over the hotspots of the drought modes.

evidence_type_annotations.claim: unknown method

Demonstrates that SPEI is a more reliable indicator than SPI for assessing drought severity in the MENA region under global warming, especially in warm, water-limited climates; reveals spatial patterns of drought intensification linked to rising temperatures in temperate and cold desert zones; quant...[truncated]
No explicit limitation extracted.
Full text excerpt:

Results indicate a significant shift from wetter conditions before 1998 to more frequent drought occurrences post-1998, particularly in northern MENA, including North Africa, the Levant, and western parts of the Arabian Peninsula, northern Iraq, and western Iran.
Both the SPI and SPEI indicate significant positive changes in drought frequency across 19.4% of the MENA region during the period 1998–2021, compared to 12.9% in the earlier period of 1981–1997.
For example, in comparison to the SPI, the SPEI indicated an increase of 15% in the areas affected by severe to very extreme droughts in arid regions (Aw).
Our findings indicate that the SPEI can be seen as a more reliable indicator for understanding drought severity in the region in the context of global warming.

method_scenario_benchmark_triples.method: Principal component analysis (PCA) applied to multi-model simulation datasets from the Coordinated Regional Climate Downscaling Experiment (CORDEX); droughts quantified using standardized precipitation evapotranspiration index (SPEI) and standardized precipitation index (SPI) at 12-month scale; model skill assessed by comparing simulated results with Climate Research Unit (CRU) observations.

Projects no change in spatial structure of drought modes but increased SPEI-12 drought intensity and frequency over hotspots under 1.5 °C and 2 °C global warming levels; identifies increased potential evapotranspiration—not reduced precipitation—as primary driver; recommends adaptation focused on re...[truncated]

Full text excerpt:
Multi-model simulation datasets from the Coordinated Regional Climate Downscaling Experiment (CORDEX) were analysed for the study.

The skill of the models to reproduce the spatial distribution and frequency of past drought modes over Eastern Africa was examined by comparing the simulated results with the Climate Research Unit (CRU) observation.
The correlation between the simulated and observed spatial pattern of the drought modes is high ( r ≥ 0.7).

Over the hotspot of the drought modes, the observed drought frequency is within the simulated values, and the simulations agree with the observation that the frequency of SPI-12 droughts is less than that of SPEI-12 droughts.

For both RCP4.5 and RCP8.5 scenarios, the simulation ensemble projects no changes in the spatial structure of the drought modes but suggests an increase in SPEI-12 drought intensity and frequency over the hotspots of the drought modes.
